topics.name: Minor Cuts
topics.description: Small cuts and scrapes

steps.instruction: Wash your hands before touching the wound.
Rinse the cut with clean running water to remove dirt and debris.
Apply gentle pressure with clean gauze until bleeding stops.
Cover the area with a sterile bandage and change it daily.

topics.name: Deeper Cuts
topics.description: Cuts that may need extra care

steps.instruction: Apply firm pressure with a clean cloth or gauze.
Elevate the injured area if possible.
If bleeding does not stop after 10 minutes, seek urgent medical care.

topics.name: Minor Burns
topics.description: Small surface burns

steps.instruction: Cool the burn under cool running water for 20 minutes.
Remove rings or tight items before swelling starts.
Cover loosely with a clean non-stick dressing.

topics.name: Severe Burns
topics.description: Large, deep, or blistering burns

steps.instruction: Call emergency services for large or deep burns.
Do not apply ice, butter, or ointments to the burned area.
Cover with a clean cloth and monitor for shock.

topics.name: Ankle Sprain
topics.description: Swelling or pain after twisting the ankle

steps.instruction: Rest the ankle and avoid putting weight on it.
Apply ice wrapped in cloth for 15 to 20 minutes at a time.
Use a compression wrap if it does not cause extra pain.
Elevate the foot above heart level when possible.

topics.name: Wrist Sprain
topics.description: Pain and tenderness after a fall or strain

steps.instruction: Stop using the wrist and rest it in a comfortable position.
Apply ice and a light compression bandage.
Seek medical assessment if the wrist looks deformed or pain is severe.

topics.name: Adult Choking
topics.description: A conscious adult unable to cough or speak

steps.instruction: Ask if the person can speak or cough.
If they cannot breathe, give five back blows between the shoulder blades.
Follow with five abdominal thrusts and repeat until the object clears.
Call emergency services if the airway does not clear quickly.

topics.name: Child Choking
topics.description: Choking response for children

steps.instruction: Keep the child calm and check if they can cough.
Give age-appropriate back blows and thrusts if the airway is blocked.
Call emergency services immediately if breathing is impaired.

topics.name: Home Care
topics.description: Steps for mild fever management

steps.instruction: Drink plenty of fluids and rest.
Wear light clothing and keep the room comfortably cool.
Use fever medicine only as directed on the label or by a clinician.

topics.name: Warning Signs
topics.description: When fever needs medical help

steps.instruction: Seek care for trouble breathing, confusion, or a stiff neck.
Get help for fever that lasts several days or keeps returning.
Watch closely for dehydration, especially in children and older adults.

categories.name: Frostbite
categories.description: Cold injury to skin and tissue

topics.name: Early Frostbite
topics.description: Numb, red, or tingling skin from cold

steps.instruction: Get the person out of the cold and into a warm place.
Remove wet clothing and any jewelry from the affected area.
Soak the area in warm (not hot) water around 100-105°F until it feels warm again.
Do not rub the skin or use direct heat from a stove or fire.

topics.name: Deep Frostbite
topics.description: Hard, white, or waxy skin with blisters

steps.instruction: Call emergency services right away.
Do not rewarm the area if it might refreeze before getting help.
Protect the area with a loose, dry covering and avoid walking on frostbitten feet.

topics.name: Mild Hypothermia
topics.description: Shivering, cold skin, and clumsy movement

steps.instruction: Move the person to a warm, dry place.
Remove any wet clothing and replace with dry layers or blankets.
If alert, give warm, sweet, non-alcoholic drinks.
Keep them still and avoid rough handling.

topics.name: Severe Hypothermia
topics.description: Confusion, weak pulse, or unresponsive

steps.instruction: Call emergency services immediately.
Do not give food or drink to anyone who is not fully alert.
Wrap them gently in blankets and keep them lying still.
If they are not breathing normally, begin CPR.

categories.name: Heat Exhaustion
categories.description: Body overheating from heat or exertion

topics.name: Cooling Down
topics.description: Heavy sweating with pale, clammy skin

steps.instruction: Move the person to a cool, shaded, or air-conditioned place.
Loosen tight clothing and apply cool, wet cloths to the skin.
Have them sip cool water or a sports drink slowly.
Let them rest until symptoms fully resolve.

topics.name: When to Get Help
topics.description: Signs that go beyond heat exhaustion

steps.instruction: Call for help if symptoms last longer than one hour or get worse.
Get emergency care if the person becomes confused or faints.
Treat as heat stroke if the skin becomes hot and the person stops sweating.

topics.name: Emergency Care
topics.description: Hot skin, confusion, or unresponsiveness

steps.instruction: Call 911 immediately.
Move the person to a cool place out of the sun.
Cool them quickly with cold wet cloths or ice packs at the neck, armpits, and groin.
Do not give fluids if they are not fully awake and able to swallow.

topics.name: F.A.S.T. Check
topics.description: How to recognize a stroke

steps.instruction: Face: ask the person to smile and look for one side drooping.
Arms: ask them to raise both arms and watch for one drifting down.
Speech: listen for slurred or strange-sounding words.
Time: call 911 right away and note the time symptoms began.

topics.name: While Waiting for Help
topics.description: Care after calling 911

steps.instruction: Keep the person calm and lying down with the head slightly raised.
Do not give them food, drink, or medication.
Stay with them and watch their breathing until help arrives.

topics.name: During a Seizure
topics.description: Keeping the person safe

steps.instruction: Move sharp or hard objects away from the person.
Place something soft under their head if possible.
Do not hold them down or put anything in their mouth.
Time the seizure from start to finish.

topics.name: After a Seizure
topics.description: Care once the seizure ends

steps.instruction: Roll them gently onto their side in the recovery position.
Stay with them and reassure them as they wake up.
Call 911 if it lasted longer than 5 minutes, repeats, or it is their first seizure.

topics.name: Adult CPR
topics.description: For unresponsive adults not breathing normally

steps.instruction: Check the scene for safety, then check responsiveness and call 911.
Place the heel of one hand on the center of the chest and stack your other hand on top.
Push hard and fast: at least 2 inches deep, 100-120 compressions per minute.
Give 30 compressions followed by 2 rescue breaths and repeat.
Use an AED as soon as one is available and follow its prompts.

topics.name: Hands-Only CPR
topics.description: If you are untrained or unable to give breaths

steps.instruction: Call 911 first.
Place both hands on the center of the chest and push hard and fast.
Aim for 100-120 compressions per minute and let the chest rise fully between pushes.
Continue until help arrives or the person starts breathing on their own.

topics.name: Bee or Wasp Sting
topics.description: Painful sting with possible swelling

steps.instruction: Scrape the stinger out with a flat-edged object like a credit card.
Wash the area with soap and water.
Apply a cold pack to reduce pain and swelling.
Watch closely for signs of a severe allergic reaction.

topics.name: Tick Bite
topics.description: A tick attached to the skin

steps.instruction: Use fine-tipped tweezers to grasp the tick close to the skin.
Pull straight up with steady, even pressure until it releases.
Clean the area with soap and water or rubbing alcohol.
Watch for a rash or flu-like symptoms in the following weeks.

topics.name: Spider Bite
topics.description: Most spider bites are minor, but some are dangerous

steps.instruction: Wash the bite with soap and water.
Apply a cold pack to reduce swelling.
Get emergency care if the spider may have been a black widow or brown recluse.
Watch for severe pain, muscle cramps, or trouble breathing.

categories.name: Shock
categories.description: Body not getting enough blood flow

topics.name: Recognizing Shock
topics.description: Pale, cool skin with rapid pulse

steps.instruction: Look for pale, cool, clammy skin and rapid breathing or heart rate.
Watch for confusion, weakness, or sudden sleepiness.
Treat any obvious cause first, such as severe bleeding or an allergic reaction.

topics.name: Treating Shock
topics.description: Care while waiting for help

steps.instruction: Call 911 immediately.
Have the person lie flat on their back if no spinal injury is suspected.
Keep them warm with a blanket and reassure them to stay calm.
Do not give them food or drink.

categories.name: Dehydration
categories.description: Not enough fluids in the body

topics.name: Rehydrating
topics.description: Steps for mild to moderate dehydration

steps.instruction: Move to a cool place and rest.
Sip water or an oral rehydration drink slowly and steadily.
Avoid alcohol, coffee, and very sugary drinks.
Get medical care if symptoms are severe or do not improve.

topics.name: During Vomiting
topics.description: Keeping the person safe

steps.instruction: Help them lean forward or roll onto their side to protect the airway.
Stay with them and offer reassurance.
Do not give fluids until vomiting has stopped.

topics.name: After Vomiting
topics.description: Recovery and rehydration

steps.instruction: Help them rinse their mouth with water.
Start with small sips of water or an oral rehydration drink.
Get medical help if there is blood in the vomit, severe pain, or signs of dehydration.

topics.name: Closed Fracture
topics.description: Pain, swelling, or deformity without an open wound

steps.instruction: Keep the injured area still and supported.
Apply a cold pack wrapped in a thin cloth to reduce swelling.
Do not try to straighten or push the bone back into place.
Get medical care to confirm and treat the injury.

topics.name: Open Fracture
topics.description: Bone visible through a wound or breaking the skin

steps.instruction: Call 911 immediately.
Control bleeding gently around the wound without pressing on the bone.
Cover the wound loosely with a clean dressing.
Keep the area still and treat for shock if needed.

topics.name: Spine or Neck Injury
topics.description: Suspected back, neck, or head injury

steps.instruction: Call 911 right away and do not move the person.
Support their head and neck in the position you found them.
Only move them if they are in immediate danger from fire or other hazards.

topics.name: Mild Reaction
topics.description: Itchy skin, sneezing, or small rash

steps.instruction: Move the person away from the suspected trigger.
Wash any contact area with soap and water.
Consider an over-the-counter antihistamine if appropriate for them.
Watch closely for any sign that symptoms are getting worse.

topics.name: Anaphylaxis
topics.description: Severe, life-threatening allergic reaction

steps.instruction: Call 911 immediately.
Help the person use their epinephrine auto-injector in the outer thigh.
Have them lie flat with legs raised, unless this makes breathing harder.
Be ready to begin CPR if they stop breathing normally.
